sentences.originalText:
_The Necklace_

She was one of those pretty and charming girls who are sometimes, as if
by a mistake of destiny, born in a family of clerks.
sentences.simplifiedText: Mathilde Loisel is a pretty girl, but her family is poor.

sentences.originalText:
She had no dowry,
no expectations, no means of being known, understood, loved, wedded, by
any rich and distinguished man; and she let herself be married to a
little clerk at the Ministry of Public Instruction.
sentences.simplifiedText: She has no money, so she marries a small clerk.

sentences.originalText:
She dressed plainly because she could not dress well, but she was as
unhappy as though she had really fallen from her proper station; since
with women there is neither caste nor rank; and beauty, grace, and
charm act instead of family and birth.
sentences.simplifiedText: She wears simple clothes, but she wants to be rich.

sentences.originalText:
Natural fineness, instinct for
what is elegant, suppleness of wit, are the sole hierarchy, and make
from women of the people the equals of the very greatest ladies.
sentences.simplifiedText: She thinks beauty and charm are more important than family.

sentences.originalText:
She suffered ceaselessly, feeling herself born for all the delicacies
and all the luxuries.
sentences.simplifiedText: She feels sad because she wants a better life.

sentences.originalText:
She suffered from the poverty of her dwelling,
from the wretched look of the walls, from the worn-out chairs, from the
ugliness of the curtains.
sentences.simplifiedText: Her home is small, and the furniture is old.

sentences.originalText:
All those things, of which another woman of
her rank would never even have been conscious, tortured her and made
her angry.
sentences.simplifiedText: These things make her unhappy and angry.

sentences.originalText:
The sight of the little Breton peasant who did her humble
housework aroused in her regrets which were despairing, and distracted
dreams.
sentences.simplifiedText: Her maid makes her think of her lost dreams.

sentences.originalText:
She thought of the silent antechambers hung with Oriental
tapestry, lit by tall bronze candelabra, and of the two great footmen
in knee breeches who sleep in the big armchairs, made drowsy by the
heavy warmth of the hot-air stove.
sentences.simplifiedText: She dreams of big rooms with beautiful decorations.

sentences.originalText:
She thought of the long
_salons_ fatted up with ancient silk, of the delicate furniture
carrying priceless curiosities, and of the coquettish perfumed boudoirs
made for talks at five o'clock with intimate friends, with men famous
and sought after, whom all women envy and whose attention they all
desire.
sentences.simplifiedText: She wants to meet important people and have nice talks.

sentences.originalText:
When she sat down to dinner, before the round table covered with a
tablecloth three days old, opposite her husband, who uncovered the soup
tureen and declared with an enchanted air, "Ah, the good
_pot-au-feu_!
sentences.simplifiedText: Mathilde sat at the table with her husband.

sentences.originalText:
I don't know anything better than that," she thought
of dainty dinners, of shining silverware, of tapestry which peopled the
walls with ancient personages and with strange birds flying in the
midst of a fairy forest; and she thought of delicious dishes served on
marvelous plates, and of the whispered gallantries which you listen to
with a sphinx-like smile, while you are eating the pink flesh of a
trout or the wings of a quail.
sentences.simplifiedText: He said, 'Ah, the good soup!' with a happy face.

sentences.originalText: She had no dresses, no jewels, nothing.
sentences.simplifiedText: She thought about fancy dinners and nice plates.

sentences.originalText:
And she loved nothing but that;
she felt made for that.
sentences.simplifiedText: She wanted to hear nice words while eating good food.

sentences.originalText:
She would so have liked to please, to be
envied, to be charming, to be sought after.
sentences.simplifiedText: She had no nice dresses or jewels.

sentences.originalText:
She had a friend, a former schoolmate at the convent, who was rich, and
whom she did not like to go and see any more, because she suffered so
much when she came back.
sentences.simplifiedText: She loved beautiful things and wanted to be admired.

sentences.originalText:
But, one evening, her husband returned home with a triumphant air, and
holding a large envelope in his hand.
sentences.simplifiedText: Mathilde had a rich friend but did not visit her.

sentences.originalText: "There," said he, "here is something for you.
sentences.simplifiedText: Seeing her friend made Mathilde feel sad.

sentences.originalText:
"

She tore the paper sharply, and drew out a printed card which bore
these words:

"The Minister of Public Instruction and Mme.
sentences.simplifiedText: One day, her husband came home with an envelope.

sentences.originalText:
Georges Ramponneau request
the honor of M.
sentences.simplifiedText: He said, 'Here is something for you.'

sentences.originalText: and Mme.
sentences.simplifiedText: Mme. Loisel and her husband get an invitation.

sentences.originalText:
Loisel's company at the palace of the Ministry
on Monday evening, January 18th.
sentences.simplifiedText: It is for a party at the Ministry in January.

sentences.originalText:
"

Instead of being delighted, as her husband hoped, she threw the
invitation on the table with disdain, murmuring:

"What do you want me to do with that?
sentences.simplifiedText: Her husband thinks she will be happy, but she is not.

sentences.originalText:
"

"But, my dear, I thought you would be glad.
sentences.simplifiedText: She throws the invitation on the table and asks, 'What do I do with this?'

sentences.originalText:
You never go out, and this
is such a fine opportunity.
sentences.simplifiedText: Her husband says, 'I thought you would be glad.'

sentences.originalText: I had awful trouble to get it.
sentences.simplifiedText: He tells her, 'You never go out, and this is a good chance.'

sentences.originalText:
Everyone
wants to go; it is very select, and they are not giving many
invitations to clerks.
sentences.simplifiedText: He worked hard to get the invitation.

sentences.originalText: The whole official world will be there.
sentences.simplifiedText: He says, 'Everyone wants to go, and it is very special.'

sentences.originalText:
"

She looked at him with an irritated eye, and she said, impatiently:

"And what do you want me to put on my back?
sentences.simplifiedText: She looks at him and asks, 'What do I wear?'

sentences.originalText:
"

He had not thought of that; he stammered:

"Why, the dress you go to the theater in.
sentences.simplifiedText: He did not think about that and says, 'Wear your theater dress.'

sentences.originalText: It looks very well, to me.
sentences.simplifiedText: It looks very nice to me.

sentences.originalText:
"

He stopped, distracted, seeing that his wife was crying.
sentences.simplifiedText: He stopped because he saw his wife crying.

sentences.originalText:
Two great
tears descended slowly from the corners of her eyes toward the corners
of her mouth.
sentences.simplifiedText: Two big tears fell from her eyes to her mouth.

sentences.originalText:
He stuttered:

"What's the matter?
sentences.simplifiedText: He asked, 'What is wrong?'

sentences.originalText: What's the matter?
sentences.simplifiedText: 'What is wrong?'

sentences.originalText:
"

But, by a violent effort, she had conquered her grief, and she replied,
with a calm voice, while she wiped her wet cheeks:

"Nothing.
sentences.simplifiedText: She tried hard and said, 'Nothing,' as she wiped her tears.

sentences.originalText: Only I have no dress, and therefore I can't go to this ball.
sentences.simplifiedText: 'I have no dress, so I can't go to the ball.'

sentences.originalText: Give your card to some colleague whose wife is better equipped than I.
sentences.simplifiedText: 'Give your card to a friend whose wife has a dress.'

sentences.originalText:
"

He was in despair.
sentences.simplifiedText: He felt very sad.

sentences.originalText:
He resumed:

"Come, let us see, Mathilde.
sentences.simplifiedText: He said, 'Come, let us see, Mathilde.'

sentences.originalText:
How much would it cost, a suitable dress,
which you could use on other occasions, something very simple?
sentences.simplifiedText: How much does a simple dress cost?

sentences.originalText:
"

She reflected several seconds, making her calculations and wondering
also what sum she could ask without drawing on herself an immediate
refusal and a frightened exclamation from the economical clerk.
sentences.simplifiedText: She thought for a moment and did some math.

sentences.originalText:
Finally, she replied, hesitatingly:

"I don't know exactly, but I think I could manage it with four hundred
francs.
sentences.simplifiedText: She did not want him to say no.

sentences.originalText:
"

He had grown a little pale, because he was laying aside just that
amount to buy a gun and treat himself to a little shooting next summer
on the plain of Nanterre, with several friends who went to shoot larks
down there of a Sunday.
sentences.simplifiedText: She said, 'I think I need four hundred francs.'

sentences.originalText:
But he said:

"All right.
sentences.simplifiedText: He looked a little pale and worried.

sentences.originalText: I will give you four hundred francs.
sentences.simplifiedText: He wanted to buy a gun with that money.

sentences.originalText:
And try to have a
pretty dress.
sentences.simplifiedText: But he said, 'Okay, I will give you four hundred francs.'

sentences.originalText:
"

The day of the ball drew near, and Mme.
sentences.simplifiedText: He told her to get a nice dress.

sentences.originalText:
Loisel seemed sad, uneasy,
anxious.
sentences.simplifiedText: The day of the ball was near, and Mme. Loisel was sad.

sentences.originalText: Her dress was ready, however.
sentences.simplifiedText: Her dress was ready, but she was still worried.

sentences.originalText:
Her husband said to her one
evening:

"What is the matter?
sentences.simplifiedText: Her husband asks, 'What is wrong?'

sentences.originalText: Come, you've been so queer these last three days.
sentences.simplifiedText: He says, 'You are strange these days.'

sentences.originalText:
"

And she answered:

"It annoys me not to have a single jewel, not a single stone, nothing
to put on.
sentences.simplifiedText: She says, 'I have no jewels to wear.'

sentences.originalText: I shall look like distress.
sentences.simplifiedText: She feels sad and says, 'I look bad.'

sentences.originalText:
I should almost rather not go at
all.
sentences.simplifiedText: She thinks, 'I do not want to go.'

sentences.originalText:
"

He resumed:

"You might wear natural flowers.
sentences.simplifiedText: He says, 'You can wear flowers.'

sentences.originalText:
It's very stylish at this time of the
year.
sentences.simplifiedText: He adds, 'Flowers are nice now.'

sentences.originalText: For ten francs you can get two or three magnificent roses.
sentences.simplifiedText: He says, 'You can buy roses for ten francs.'

sentences.originalText:
"

She was not convinced.
sentences.simplifiedText: She does not agree with him.

sentences.originalText:
"No; there's nothing more humiliating than to look poor among other
women who are rich.
sentences.simplifiedText: She says, 'I feel poor next to rich women.'

sentences.originalText:
"

But her husband cried:

"How stupid you are!
sentences.simplifiedText: But her husband said, 'You are silly and wrong!'

sentences.originalText: Go look up your friend Mme.
sentences.simplifiedText: Go to your friend Mme. Forestier, and ask her for jewels.

sentences.originalText:
Forestier, and ask her
to lend you some jewels.
sentences.simplifiedText: You are close enough to her to ask.

sentences.originalText:
You're quite thick enough with her to do
that.
sentences.simplifiedText: She was happy and said, 'It is true!'

sentences.originalText:
"

She uttered a cry of joy:

"It's true.
sentences.simplifiedText: I never thought of this before.

sentences.originalText: I never thought of it.
sentences.simplifiedText: The next day, she went to her friend and talked about her problem.

sentences.originalText:
"

The next day she went to her friend and told of her distress.
sentences.simplifiedText: Mme. Forestier went to a wardrobe and took out a big jewel box.

sentences.originalText: Mme.
sentences.simplifiedText: She brought it back and opened it.

sentences.originalText:
Forestier went to a wardrobe with a glass door, took out a large
jewel box, brought it back, opened it, and said to Mme.
sentences.simplifiedText: Mme. Forestier said to Mme. Loisel, 'Choose, my dear.'

sentences.originalText:
Loisel:

"Choose, my dear.
sentences.simplifiedText: Mme. Loisel was excited and looked at the jewels.

sentences.originalText:
"

She saw first of all some bracelets, then a pearl necklace, then a
Venetian cross, gold and precious stones of admirable workmanship.
sentences.simplifiedText: Mathilde saw some bracelets and a pearl necklace.

sentences.originalText:
She
tried on the ornaments before the glass, hesitated, could not make up
her mind to part with them, to give them back.
sentences.simplifiedText: She also saw a cross with gold and stones.

sentences.originalText:
She kept asking:

"Haven't you any more?
sentences.simplifiedText: She tried on the jewelry and did not want to return it.

sentences.originalText:
"

"Why, yes.
sentences.simplifiedText: She asked, 'Do you have more?'

sentences.originalText: Look.
sentences.simplifiedText: 'Yes, look here,' her friend said.

sentences.originalText: I don't know what you like.
sentences.simplifiedText: 'I don't know what you like.'

sentences.originalText:
"

All of a sudden she discovered, in a black satin box, a superb necklace
of diamonds, and her heart began to beat with an immoderate desire.
sentences.simplifiedText: Mathilde found a diamond necklace in a black box.

sentences.originalText:
Her
hands trembled as she took it.
sentences.simplifiedText: Her heart beat fast, and her hands shook.

sentences.originalText:
She fastened it around her throat,
outside her high-necked dress, and remained lost in ecstasy at the
sight of herself.
sentences.simplifiedText: She put it on and loved how she looked.

sentences.originalText:
Then she asked, hesitating, filled with anguish:

"Can you lend me that, only that?
sentences.simplifiedText: She asked, 'Can you lend me this necklace?'

sentences.originalText:
"

"Why, yes, certainly.
sentences.simplifiedText: Mathilde said, 'Yes, of course.'

sentences.originalText:
"

She sprang upon the neck of her friend, kissed her passionately, then
fled with her treasure.
sentences.simplifiedText: She hugged her friend and kissed her, then left quickly.

sentences.originalText:
* * * * *

The day of the ball arrived.
sentences.simplifiedText: The day of the ball came.

sentences.originalText: Mme.
sentences.simplifiedText: Mme. Loisel was very successful.

sentences.originalText: Loisel made a great success.
sentences.simplifiedText: She was prettier than everyone and very happy.

sentences.originalText:
She was
prettier than them all, elegant, gracious, smiling, and crazy with joy.
sentences.simplifiedText: All the men looked at her and asked her name.

sentences.originalText: All the men looked at her, asked her name, endeavored to be introduced.
sentences.simplifiedText: They wanted to meet her and dance with her.

sentences.originalText: All the attachés of the Cabinet wanted to waltz with her.
sentences.simplifiedText: Even the minister noticed her.

sentences.originalText:
She was
remarked by the minister himself.
sentences.simplifiedText: She danced with joy and forgot everything.

sentences.originalText:
She danced with intoxication, with passion, made drunk by pleasure,
forgetting all, in the triumph of her beauty, in the glory of her
success, in a sort of cloud of happiness composed of all this homage,
of all this admiration, of all these awakened desires, and of that
sense of complete victory which is so sweet to woman's heart.
sentences.simplifiedText: She felt happy and proud of her beauty and success.

sentences.originalText: She went away about four o'clock in the morning.
sentences.simplifiedText: Mathilde left the party at four in the morning.

sentences.originalText:
Her husband had been
sleeping since midnight, in a little deserted anteroom, with three
other gentlemen whose wives were having a very good time.
sentences.simplifiedText: Her husband was sleeping in a small room with other men.

sentences.originalText:
He threw over her shoulders the wraps which he had brought, modest
wraps of common life, whose poverty contrasted with the elegance of the
ball dress.
sentences.simplifiedText: He put a simple coat on her shoulders.

sentences.originalText:
She felt this and wanted to escape so as not to be remarked
by the other women, who were enveloping themselves in costly furs.
sentences.simplifiedText: She felt poor and wanted to leave quickly.

sentences.originalText: Loisel held her back.
sentences.simplifiedText: Loisel stopped her and said,

sentences.originalText: "Wait a bit.
sentences.simplifiedText: "Wait a bit.

sentences.originalText: You will catch cold outside.
sentences.simplifiedText: You will get cold outside.

sentences.originalText: I will go and call a cab.
sentences.simplifiedText: I will find a cab."

sentences.originalText:
"

But she did not listen to him, and rapidly descended the stairs.
sentences.simplifiedText: But Mathilde did not listen and ran down the stairs.

sentences.originalText:
When
they were in the street they did not find a carriage; and they began to
look for one, shouting after the cabmen whom they saw passing by at a
distance.
sentences.simplifiedText: They could not find a cab, so they shouted for one.

sentences.originalText: They went down toward the Seine, in despair, shivering with cold.
sentences.simplifiedText: They walked to the Seine and felt cold.

sentences.originalText:
At
last they found on the quay one of those ancient noctambulent coupés
which, exactly as if they were ashamed to show their misery during the
day, are never seen round Paris until after nightfall.
sentences.simplifiedText: They found an old car by the river.

sentences.originalText:
It took them to their door in the Rue des Martyrs, and once more,
sadly, they climbed up homeward.
sentences.simplifiedText: The car took them to their home in Rue des Martyrs.

sentences.originalText: All was ended for her.
sentences.simplifiedText: They went inside and felt very sad.

sentences.originalText:
And as to him,
he reflected that he must be at the Ministry at ten o'clock.
sentences.simplifiedText: He thought he must go to work at ten.

sentences.originalText:
She removed the wraps, which covered her shoulders, before the glass,
so as once more to see herself in all her glory.
sentences.simplifiedText: She took off her wraps and looked in the mirror.

sentences.originalText:
But suddenly she
uttered a cry.
sentences.simplifiedText: Suddenly, she cried out loud.

sentences.originalText: She had no longer the necklace around her neck!
sentences.simplifiedText: The necklace was not on her neck!

sentences.originalText:
Her husband, already half undressed, demanded:

"What is the matter with you?
sentences.simplifiedText: Her husband was taking off his clothes.

sentences.originalText:
"

She turned madly toward him:

"I have--I have--I've lost Mme.
sentences.simplifiedText: He asked, 'What is wrong with you?'
